ontology.foodex2_description: Wonton soup — composite dish of filled dumplings in broth
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No dedicated FoodEx2 code for vegetable-pork wonton soup; A0CKP (composite soup dish) used as closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for wonton soup as a specific food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of wonton soup (approximately 350 g) containing 6–8 wontons in chicken broth, as typically served at a Singapore hawker centre.

nutrient_source.title: 菜肉馄饨汤 Vegetable & Pork Wantons Served In Superior Chicken Broth
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) — Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for 菜肉馄饨汤 Vegetable & Pork Wantons Served In Superior Chicken Broth (cr_id G00010036). All per-100g values are sourced verbatim from this record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson et al.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for vegetable and pork wonton soup. The HPB source reports carbohydrate_g as 0.0 per 100g for this dish entry, which likely reflects the broth-dominant composition of the analysed sample rather than the true whole-dish carbohydrate content (wonton wrappers contain refined wheat flour). A GI of 55 is assigned as an analogous estimate based on wheat-flour-based dumplings/pasta (GI ~47–65 range per Atkinson et al. 2008 and University of Sydney GI Database for pasta and wheat-based products). Glycemic load per serving is calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100; because the HPB-reported carbohydrate is 0.0 g/100g, GL computes to 0.0 — this is a data artefact of the HPB entry and is documented accordingly. Consumers should be aware that wonton wrappers do contribute refined carbohydrates in practice.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1050 mg per 100 g — well above the 600 mg/100 g high-concern threshold
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish; if consumed, request reduced-sodium broth, avoid drinking the soup, and count this dish against the daily sodium target of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Wonton wrappers are made from refined wheat flour, contributing rapidly absorbed carbohydrates with negligible dietary fibre
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to reduce the refined carbohydrate load from wonton wrappers, pair the dish with non-starchy vegetables, and monitor post-meal blood glucose, as the wheat-flour wrappers can raise glycaemic response despite the broth-dominant composition.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium 1050 mg/100 g and cholesterol 89 mg/100 g, with pork fat contributing saturated fatty acids
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish due to its very high sodium content (1050 mg/100 g) and cholesterol load (89 mg/100 g); if consumed, avoid drinking the broth, request leaner fillings, and ensure the meal fits within daily saturated fat and sodium limits.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pork is rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6), amplifying inflammatory cytokine production; sodium at 1050 mg/100 g worsens RA-related tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should consider substituting pork filling with chicken or tofu, request reduced seasoning to lower sodium intake, and limit consumption frequency to minimise pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid exposure.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Vegetable and Pork Wonton Soup (菜肉馄饨汤) is a classic Chinese soup dish widely served at Singapore hawker centres and Chinese restaurants. It consists of thin wheat-flour wonton wrappers filled with a mixture of ground pork and chopped vegetables — typically cabbage, chives, or water chestnuts — folded into small parcels and boiled until tender. The wontons are served in a clear, savoury superior chicken broth seasoned with soy sauce, sesame oil, and ginger, producing a light yet flavourful soup. The texture contrasts silky, slightly chewy wonton skin with a juicy, well-seasoned pork-and-vegetable filling. Nutritionally, the HPB Singapore database records 171 kcal, 19.1 g protein, and 10.5 g fat per 100 g, with sodium at a clinically significant 1050 mg/100 g — driven by the seasoned broth and soy sauce. Cholesterol is 89 mg/100 g from the pork filling. The dish is not suitable for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease due to its very high sodium content, and those with Type 2 Diabetes should be mindful of the refined carbohydrate contribution from the wonton wrappers. It is not halal due to the pork content. A standard hawker serving is approximately 350 g (one bowl containing 6–8 wontons).

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (cr_id G00010036), which is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. The match is exact — the HPB entry corresponds precisely to this dish. Confidence is set to high.
notes: carbohydrate_g is reported as 0.0 per 100g by the HPB source for this dish entry. This is an anomaly likely reflecting the broth-dominant composition of the analysed sample or a data gap in the HPB record; wonton wrappers are made from refined wheat flour and do contribute carbohydrates in practice. The value is preserved as reported by the approved source (confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Food Composition Database G00010036), but consumers and clinicians should be aware that actual carbohydrate content per serving will be higher than 0.0 g/100g suggests. sugar_g is 0.0 per HPB — confirmed zero per HPB source. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 per HPB — confirmed zero per HPB source. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown not reported in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for this dish. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for this dish as of 2026-04-16. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on wheat-flour-based pasta/dumpling products per Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for this dish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GL per serving is 0.0 due to the HPB-reported carbohydrate_g of 0.0 — this is a data artefact and is documented above. food_subgroup corrected from 'Chicken-based dishes' to 'Pork-based dishes' as the primary filling ingredient is pork; chicken broth is the cooking medium only. According to the University of Sydney GI Database, a GI of 55 falls into the 'low' category (GI <= 55).

improvement_actions_taken: Corrected ontology.foodex2_match_type from 'analogous_estimate' to 'closest_parent' to align with allowed values.

Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' to accurately reflect a GI of 55 according to the University of Sydney GI Database guidelines (GI <= 55 is low). Added a note to the 'notes' field to justify this change.
